part.text:
# Basic Trigger.dev Task Setup

## Background

Initialize a new Trigger.dev project and create a simple task that logs a message.

## Requirements

* Initialize a Trigger.dev project in `/home/user/my-project` using `npx trigger.dev@latest init -p $TRIGGER_PROJECT_REF`.
* Create a task in `/home/user/my-project/trigger/hello-world.ts`.
* The task ID must be `hello-world-${trial_id}`, where `trial_id` is read from `/logs/trial_id`.
* The task should accept a payload with a `message` string and log it using `console.log`.
* The task should return an object `{ success: true, echoed: payload.message }`.

## Implementation Guide

1. Read `trial_id` from `/logs/trial_id`.
2. Run `npx trigger.dev@latest init -p $TRIGGER_PROJECT_REF` (select default options if prompted, but usually `-p` handles it).
3. Create the file `trigger/hello-world.ts` with the task definition.
4. Ensure the task ID is correctly suffixed with `trial_id`.

## Constraints

* Project path: /home/user/my-project
* Use Trigger.dev V4 SDK (`@trigger.dev/sdk`).

## Integrations

* Trigger.dev

# Basic Trigger.dev Task Setup

## Background

Initialize a new Trigger.dev project and create a simple task that logs a message.

## Requirements

* Initialize a Trigger.dev project in `/home/user/my-project` using `npx trigger.dev@latest init -p $TRIGGER_PROJECT_REF`.
* Create a task in `/home/user/my-project/trigger/hello-world.ts`.
* The task ID must be `hello-world-${trial_id}`, where `trial_id` is read from `/logs/trial_id`.
* The task should accept a payload with a `message` string and log it using `console.log`.
* The task should return an object `{ success: true, echoed: payload.message }`.

## Implementation Guide

1. Read `trial_id` from `/logs/trial_id`.
2. Run `npx trigger.dev@latest init -p $TRIGGER_PROJECT_REF` (select default options if prompted, but usually `-p` handles it).
3. Create the file `trigger/hello-world.ts` with the task definition.
4. Ensure the task ID is correctly suffixed with `trial_id`.

## Constraints

* Project path: /home/user/my-project
* Use Trigger.dev V4 SDK (`@trigger.dev/sdk`).

## Integrations

* Trigger.dev

Initialize your existing project for development with Trigger.dev

Arguments:
  path                             The path to the project (default: ".")

Options:
  -p, --project-ref <project ref>  The project ref to use when initializing the
                                   project
  --javascript                     Initialize the project with JavaScript
                                   instead of TypeScript (default: false)
  -t, --tag <package tag>          The version of the @trigger.dev/sdk package
                                   to install (default: "4.4.4")
  -r, --runtime <runtime>          Which runtime to use for the project.
                                   Currently only supports node and bun
                                   (default: "node")
  --skip-package-install           Skip installing the @trigger.dev/sdk package
  --override-config                Override the existing config file if it
                                   exists
  --pkg-args <args>                Additional arguments to pass to the package
                                   manager, accepts CSV for multiple args
  -y, --yes                        Skip all prompts and use defaults (requires
                                   --project-ref)
  --profile <profile>              The login profile to use (default:
                                   "default")
  -a, --api-url <value>            Override the API URL (default:
                                   "https://api.trigger.dev")
  -l, --log-level <level>          The CLI log level to use (debug, info, log,

**Examining the Flags**

I'm currently trying to understand the nuances of the `-y` flag in the context of project initialization. It seems I might have jumped the gun by using `-y` with an empty directory. The documentation suggests that `-y` is designed to be used with an existing project. I'll need to look more closely at the initialization process and existing project structure to get this right.

**Examining the Flags**

I'm currently trying to understand the nuances of the `-y` flag in the context of project initialization. It seems I might have jumped the gun by using `-y` with an empty directory. The documentation suggests that `-y` is designed to be used with an existing project. I'll need to look more closely at the initialization process and existing project structure to get this right.


**Testing the Assumptions**

I've realized `-y` probably failed because the directory wasn't a pre-existing project. The documentation and the nature of Trigger.dev V4 support an "existing" project. So, the next step involves using `npm init -y` to set up the project structure first. After that, I'll retry the `--yes` flag with the existing project, as the documentation intends.

content.text:
# CLI init command - Trigger.dev
created: 2026-01-29, last updated: 2026-03-06, [Read more](https://trigger.dev/docs/cli-init-commands)

Run the command like this:
```
npx trigger.dev@latest init
```
## ​ Options
​
Javascript
--javascript
By default, the init command assumes you are using TypeScript.
Use this flag to initialize a project that uses JavaScript.
​
Project ref
--project-ref | -p
The project ref to use when initializing the project.
​
Package tag
--tag | -t
The version of the `@trigger.dev/sdk` package to install.
Defaults to `latest`.
...
### ​ Common options
These options are available on most commands.
​
Login profile
--profile
The login profile to use.
Defaults to “default”.
​
API URL
--api-url | -a
Override the default API URL.
If not specified, it uses `https://api.trigger.dev`.
This can also be set via the `TRIGGER_API_URL` environment variable.
​
Log level
--log-level | -l
The CLI log level to use.
Options are `debug`, `info`, `log`, `warn`, `error`, and `none`.
This does not affect the log level of your trigger.dev tasks.
Defaults to `log`.
​
Skip telemetry
--skip-telemetry

# Updated @trigger.dev rules for v4-beta
created: 2025-04-23, last updated: 2026-03-22, [Read more](https://gist.github.com/hongkongkiwi/547201811128a7d23dcb4a269b5f4e1c)

| |---|
|--|--|
| |globs: **/trigger/**/*.ts, **/trigger/**/*.tsx,**/trigger/**/*.js,**/trigger/**/*.jsx|
| |description: Guidelines for writing Trigger.dev tasks|
| |alwaysApply: false|
...
| |# How to write Trigger.dev tasks|
| | |
| |## Overview of writing a Trigger.dev task|
| | |
| |1.
Run the CLI `init` command: `npx trigger.dev@v4-beta init`.|
| |2.
Create a Trigger.dev task.|
| |3.
Set up any environment variables.|
...
| |### Locals API (Replacement for init)|
| | |
| |The `init` hook is deprecated.
Use the new `locals` API and middleware instead:|
| | |
| |```ts|
| |import { locals, tasks, logger } from "@trigger.dev/sdk";|
| | |
| |// Define your local type|
| |const DbLocal = locals.create<{|
| |connect: () => Promise<void>;|
| |disconnect: () => Promise<void>;|
| |}>("db");|
| | |

# @trigger.dev/init
created: 2023-06-29, last updated: 2026-03-24, [Read more](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@trigger.dev/init)

## ✨ @trigger.dev/init - Initialize your Next.js project to start using Trigger.dev
Trigger.dev is an open source platform that makes it easy to create event-driven background tasks directly your Next.js project.
## 💻 Usage
To initialize your Next.js project using
```
@trigger.dev/init
```
, run any of the following three commands and answer the prompts:
### npm
> npx @trigger.dev/init@latest
### yarn
> yarn @trigger.dev/init@latest
### pnpm
> pnpm dlx @trigger.dev/init@latest

# @trigger.dev/cli NPM | npm.io
created: 2025-01-17, last updated: 2025-02-14, [Read more](https://npm.io/package/@trigger.dev/cli)

## ✨ @trigger.dev/cli - Initialize your project to start using Trigger.dev
Trigger.dev is an open source platform that makes it easy to create event-driven background tasks directly in your existing project.
## 💻 Usage
To initialize your project using
`@trigger.dev/cli`, run any of the following three commands and answer the prompts:
### npm
`npx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init`
### yarn
`yarn dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init`
### pnpm
`pnpm dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init`

# [v4] Self-hosted tutorial bugs (with suggested fixes) #2186
created: 2025-06-19, last updated: 2026-01-01, [Read more](https://github.com/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/issues/2186)

This is mainly a `n00b user` issue, but that command has to be run in a fresh folder vs the `/hosting/docker` pwd where users will be if they're following the tutorial steps.
...
Follow v4 Docker tutorial
...
> 1.
...
I just tried with the latest main version and a fresh compose project and it worked with the defaults.
I think it's possible you have an old `.env.example` version.
It should look like this now:
trigger.dev/hosting/docker/.env.example
…
1. The `latest` package version still points to v3 until v4 goes GA next month.
You'll want to run `npx trigger.dev@v4-beta init ..` instead
2. We used to have a `--pkg-args` flag to deal with this but it looks like it's now broken.
You could add `ignore-workspace-root-check=true` to your `.npmrc` and try again.

## 💻 Usage To initialize your project using `@trigger.dev/cli`, run any of the following three commands and answer the prompts: ### npm ```sh npx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init ``` ### yarn ```sh yarn dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init ``` ### pnpm ```sh pnpm dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init ```

# GitHub - mazeway-dev/Mazeway: Clerk but you own the code because authentication should live in your project, not a node_modules folder.
created: 2024-12-08, last updated: 2025-08-13, [Read more](https://github.com/mazeway-dev/Mazeway)

We're gonna use Trigger.dev because:
...
It's not a queue
3. Create a Trigger.dev project
- Sign up for Trigger.dev
- Complete the onboarding
- Create an organization
- Choose the free plan
- Create a project
4. Init Trigger.dev in the project
- Run this in the terminal:
```
npx trigger.dev@latest init
```
- Authenticate if needed
- Select your project
- Trigger.dev directory: default
- Choose an example to create: None
5. Add the dev API key to your `.env.local`:
- Go to "API Keys" in the Trigger.dev dashboard
- They already created keys for you!
Just grab the `Dev` one

# @trigger.dev/cli
created: 2025-01-24, last updated: 2026-03-24, [Read more](https://www.npmjs.com/package/@trigger.dev/cli?activeTab=versions)

## ✨ @trigger.dev/cli - Initialize your project to start using Trigger.dev
Trigger.dev is an open source platform that makes it easy to create event-driven background tasks directly in your existing project.
## 💻 Usage
To initialize your project using
```
@trigger.dev/cli
```
, run any of the following three commands and answer the prompts:
### npm
> npx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init
### yarn
> yarn dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init
### pnpm
> pnpm dlx @trigger.dev/cli@latest init

# Quickstart - Trigger.dev - Mintlify
created: 2026-03-25, last updated: 2026-04-12, [Read more](https://mintlify.wiki/triggerdotdev/trigger.dev/quickstart)

This guide walks you through creating an account, initializing Trigger.dev in an existing project, writing a task, running it in development, and deploying it to production.
You need Node.js 18.20.0 or later and an existing TypeScript or JavaScript project (Next.js, Remix, Express, etc.).
1
…
Run the `init` command in the root of your project:
```
npx trigger.dev@latest init
```
The CLI will:- Ask you to log in (or create an account) if you’re not already authenticated
- Ask which project to connect to
- Install `@trigger.dev/sdk` as a dependency
- Create a `trigger.config.ts` configuration file
- Create a `/trigger` directory with a sample `hello-world.ts` task
Your `trigger.config.ts` will look like this:
```
import { defineConfig } from "@trigger.dev/sdk";
export default defineConfig({
project: "<your-project-ref>",
dirs: ["./trigger"],
});
```
...
Start the dev server
Run the dev command from your project root:
…
```
npx trigger.dev@latest deploy --env staging
```
